turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the last opinion this morning in case number 21-1599 Polselli v. The Internal Revenue Service. This is a very technical tax case, so I will just hit the main point. It is about the authority of the IRS to get informationabout people they have reason to believemaybe trying to avoid paying taxes. Basically speaking the IRS can get documents and other information from other people in aid of the collection of yourliability and it does not have to notify you or the other people. Now in a prior case from the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals, the court just one level below us, the court issued a decision saying this was too broad. That Court said we should read in aid of the collection to mean that the documents sought would produce actual assets of the taxpayer. The lower court in this case the Sixth Circuit disagreed and said in aid of can allow search of any documents that might lead to accounts of the taxpayers. So two courts below us could not agree. Now tax rules have to be the same Nationwide and that is why we took the case. We agree with the Sixth Circuit and affirm its decision. We think in aid of naturally means a step leading to the end you are trying to reach, not just to the final destination. Say you are going to cook dinner. Going to the grocery store would be a step in aid of that goal even though it is not the actual cooking. Now so too here. When you are trying to find accounts of the taxpayer, it might help to search for documents from say a company he had dealings with, even though it may be unlikely the taxpayers own assets could be found there. The company documents may be an important step in a paper trail to the taxpayers own accounts. The records of the company would be in aid of getting to the taxpayers' assets. Now we recognized that there must be limits to this. So in the example I just gave, even if driving to the grocery store was in aid of cooking dinner that doesn't mean that you bought your car in aid of cooking dinner. Now by analogy the IRS should generally not be able to search the documents of the car dealership. Even the government agrees that there must be some such limit, but where exactly the line should be drawn is not presented here so we do not address that issue. So we affirmed the Sixth Circuit, its rulewill apply nationwide. The opinion of the court is unanimous, Justice Jackson has filled a concurring opinion with which Justice Gorsuch agrees.
